content.text:
Memory stage: NoteConstructor v1.
        Current time: <instant>
        Timezone: Asia/Jerusalem
        Namespace: benchmark:longmemeval:6aeb4375_abs

        Router decision: MIXED
        Router memory types: CLAIM, NOTE, SOURCE
        Router reason: Forced memory write; transcript contains durable/recallable user food interests, menu choices, recent rice experimentation, and restaurant-related context plus reusable recommendations/rationale about dish pairings and rice suitability.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

        Relevant persisted memory for dedup/context only:
        - profile benchmark:longmemeval:6aeb4375_abs:profile:<0> Profile for User (USER).
Stable facts:
- The user was planning a dinner-party menu that includes Korean BBQ options paired with Indian and Middle Eastern dishes.
- The user was planning to make falafel as one of the Middle Eastern options for the dinner party.
- The user had recently tried three different Korean restaurants.
- The user tried a unique take on Japchae with a tasty sweet potato noodle at one Korean restaurant.
- The user tried an amazing bibimbap at one Korean restaurant.
- The user tried a really spicy kimchi stew at one Korean restaurant.
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-5: has_goal; The user was planning a dinner-party menu that includes Korean BBQ options paired with Indian and Middle Eastern dishes.; scope=Past chat session about planning a fusion dinner-party menu on 2023-05-28.
- note hot-path-14e52aa162ac-note-2: CONTEXT; Fusion dinner party menu direction: Korean BBQ, Indian dishes, and falafel; The user planned a dinner party menu that mixes selected Korean BBQ options with Indian dishes and Middle Eastern options, specifically falafel. Reusable menu context includes scalable crowd dishes and cross-cuisine pairing concerns rather than a single fixed recipe.; scope=User's dinner party menu planning involving Korean BBQ, Indian cuisine, Middle Eastern cuisine, and falafel
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-6: has_goal; The user was planning to make falafel as one of the Middle Eastern options for the dinner party.; scope=Past dinner-party menu planning session on 2023-05-28.
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-7: has_experience_with; The user had recently tried three different Korean restaurants.; scope=Reusable food-experience context from the 2023-05-28 chat session.
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-9: has_experience_with; The user tried a really spicy kimchi stew at one Korean restaurant.; scope=Reusable food-experience context from the 2023-05-28 chat session.
- note hot-path-14e52aa162ac-note-4: CONTEXT; User's Korean restaurant tasting context: bibimbap, kimchi stew, and Japchae; The user recently tried three Korean restaurants: one with amazing bibimbap, one with very spicy kimchi stew, and one with a unique Japchae using tasty sweet potato noodles. The user framed the restaurants as having distinct flavors and styles.; scope=User's recent Korean restaurant experiences and dish impressions
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-10: has_experience_with; The user tried a unique take on Japchae with a tasty sweet potato noodle at one Korean restaurant.; scope=Reusable food-experience context from the 2023-05-28 chat session.
- claim hot-path-14e52aa162ac-claim-8: has_experience_with; The user tried an amazing bibimbap at one Korean restaurant.; scope=Reusable food-experience context from the 2023-05-28 chat session.
- note hot-path-14e52aa162ac-note-3: CONTEXT; Korean noodle exploration options discussed for home cooking; The user's Korean noodle exploration included home-cooking candidates Naengmyeon, Ramyeon, Jjajangmyeon, Japchae, Bibim Naengmyeon, Kalguksu, and Haemultang, with emphasis on experimenting with seasonings, spice level, toppings, and high-quality Korean noodles.; scope=User's exploration of Korean noodle dishes to make at home

        Resolved entities:
        - mention=I; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<0>; type=existing; name=I; reason=First-person statements refer to the stable namespace-level User entity.
- mention=Korean-style BBQ beef; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<1>; type=existing; name=Korean-style BBQ beef; reason=Korean-style BBQ beef is a specific dish within the existing Korean BBQ concept used in the dinner-party context.
- mention=Korean restaurants; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<2>; type=existing; name=Korean restaurants; reason=The mention exactly matches the existing Korean restaurants concept.
- mention=Middle Eastern falafel; action=LINK_EXISTING; id=entity:<3>; type=existing; name=Middle Eastern falafel; reason=Falafel is discussed as one of the user's Middle Eastern options; candidate only has the broader Middle Eastern cuisine concept.
- mention=dinner party menu; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<4>; type=PROJECT; name=User's dinner party menu; reason=The menu is a reusable standalone planning initiative containing Korean, Indian, and Middle Eastern dishes.
- mention=kimchi; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<5>; type=CONCEPT; name=Kimchi; reason=Kimchi is a concrete reusable food concept selected for the menu and absent from candidates.
- mention=bokkeumbap; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<6>; type=CONCEPT; name=Bokkeumbap; reason=Bokkeumbap is a concrete reusable food concept selected for the menu and absent from candidates.
- mention=samosas; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<7>; type=CONCEPT; name=Samosas; reason=Samosas are selected for the user's menu and absent from candidates.
- mention=saag aloo; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<8>; type=CONCEPT; name=Saag aloo; reason=Saag aloo is selected for the user's menu and absent from candidates.
- mention=basmati rice; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<9>; type=CONCEPT; name=Basmati rice; reason=Basmati rice is a reusable food concept discussed as the rice choice and absent from candidates.
- mention=Japanese short-grain rice; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<10>; type=CONCEPT; name=Japanese short-grain rice; reason=The user's rice type is a reusable food concept and absent from candidates.
- mention=new Indian restaurant that just opened up in my neighborhood; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<11>; type=ORGANIZATION; name=New Indian restaurant in user's neighborhood; reason=The restaurant is a concrete local organization the user has been meaning to try, but no proper name is given.
- mention=chicken tikka masala; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<12>; type=CONCEPT; name=Chicken tikka masala; reason=Chicken tikka masala is a reusable dish concept mentioned as a specialty of the new restaurant.
- mention=current project; action=CREATE_NEW; id=entity:<13>; type=PROJECT; name=Current project; reason=Default current-project subject for claims without a more specific resolved subject

        Retrieval plan context:
        entity_queries=user|Korean-style BBQ beef|kimchi|bokkeumbap|Middle Eastern falafel|samosas|saag aloo|basmati rice|Japanese short-grain rice|Indian restaurant|Korean restaurants
        text_queries=dinner party menu Korean BBQ beef kimchi bokkeumbap falafel samosas saag aloo basmati rice|user tried four Korean restaurants looking for new recommendations|user has been trying different types of rice lately Japanese short-grain rice basmati rice|new Indian restaurant neighborhood chicken tikka masala
        predicate_hints=prefers|owns|has_experience_with|pending_pickup

Source metadata: origin=provided_text; input_kind=TEXT; source_ref=longmemeval:6aeb4375_abs:session:2; title=LongMemEval 6aeb4375_abs session 2; user_consent_confirmed=true; force_memory_write=true; mode=force
LongMemEval past chat session.
Session date: 2023/05/30 (Tue) 23:08
Transcript:
user: I'm planning a dinner party and need some help with the menu. I was thinking of making Korean-style BBQ beef, but I want to know if you have any recommendations for side dishes that would complement it well.
assistant: What a great choice! Korean-style BBQ beef is always a crowd-pleaser! I'd be happy to help you with some side dish recommendations that would complement it perfectly. Here are some traditional Korean side dishes that pair well with BBQ beef:

1. **Kimchi** (): Spicy fermented Korean cabbage that adds a sour, salty, and umami flavor to the dish. It's a classic Korean side dish that pairs perfectly with BBQ beef.
2. **Bokkeumbap** (): A simple Korean-style fried rice dish that can be made with leftover rice, vegetables, and sometimes meat. It's a great way to soak up the flavorful juices from the BBQ beef.
3. **Sigeumchi** (): Boiled spinach seasoned with garlic, sesame oil, and salt. It's a light and refreshing side dish that helps balance the richness of the beef.
4. **Gyeranjjim** (): Steamed eggs flavored with sesame oil, soy sauce, and garlic. They're creamy and savory, and their mild flavor won't overpower the beef.
5. **Oi Muchim** (): A cucumber salad dressed with vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil. It's a cooling side dish that helps cut the richness of the BBQ beef.
6. ** Japchae** (): Stir-fried glass noodles made from sweet potato starch, beef, and vegetables. It's a popular Korean side dish that pairs well with the savory flavors of BBQ beef.
7. **Kongnamul Muchim** (): Bean sprout salad seasoned with garlic, ginger, and sesame oil. It's a crunchy and refreshing side dish that complements the beef nicely.
8. **Gyeran-mari** (): Korean-style rolled omelette made with eggs, vegetables, and sometimes meat. It's a easy to make and adds a nice protein-packed option to the table.

Choose one or two of these side dishes to serve alongside your Korean-style BBQ beef, and you'll have a well-rounded and delicious meal. Enjoy your dinner party!
user: I think I'll go with kimchi and bokkeumbap. By the way, have you tried any good Korean restaurants in my city lately? I've tried four different ones so far, and I'm always looking for new recommendations.
assistant: Kimchi and bokkeumbap are a great combination to go with your Korean-style BBQ beef.

As for your question, I'm afraid I'm a large language model, I don't have personal experiences or physical presence, so I haven't tried any Korean restaurants in your city or any other location. I exist solely to provide information and assist with tasks through text-based conversations. However, I can suggest a few ways to find great Korean restaurants in your area:

1. **Online Review Sites**: Check out review platforms like Yelp, Google Reviews, or TripAdvisor to see what others have to say about Korean restaurants in your city. You can filter the results by rating, price, and location to find the best options.
2. **Food Blogs and Social Media**: Look for local food bloggers or social media influencers who specialize in Korean cuisine. They often share reviews, recommendations, and behind-the-scenes stories about their dining experiences.
3. **Korean Communities or Cultural Centers**: Reach out to local Korean communities, cultural centers, or language exchange groups. They might have recommendations or insider knowledge about authentic Korean restaurants in your area.
4. **Ask Around**: Ask your friends, colleagues, or acquaintances who have tried Korean food before for their recommendations. Personal referrals can be a great way to discover hidden gems.

Remember to always check menus, prices, and hours of operation before visiting a new restaurant to ensure it fits your preferences and schedule.

I hope these suggestions help you find an amazing Korean restaurant in your city!
user: I'm actually thinking of making some changes to my dinner party menu. Do you have any recipe ideas for Indian-inspired dishes that would complement the Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel well?
assistant: What an interesting fusion of flavors! Indian cuisine can complement the bold flavors of Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel nicely. Here are some Indian-inspired dish ideas that might work well for your dinner party:

**Appetizers**

1. **Samosas**: Crunchy fried or baked pastries filled with spiced potatoes, peas, and onions. They're a popular Indian snack that pairs well with the crispy falafel.
2. **Papri Chaat**: A refreshing street food-style snack made with crispy fried flour bread, yogurt, chickpeas, onions, and tamarind chutney. It's a great palate cleanser between bites of spicy BBQ beef.

**Sides**

1. **Basmati Rice**: A flavorful and aromatic rice dish that pairs well with the bold flavors of Korean BBQ beef and falafel. You can add saffron, cardamom, or cinnamon to give it an Indian twist.
2. **Saag Aloo**: Spinach cooked with garlic, ginger, and potatoes, flavored with cumin, coriander, and garam masala. This side dish complements the savory flavors of the beef and falafel.
3. **Raita**: A cooling yogurt-based side dish with cucumber, cumin, coriander, and cayenne pepper. It helps balance the spiciness of the Korean BBQ beef.

**Desserts**

1. **Gulab Jamun**: Deep-fried dumplings soaked in a sweet and fragrant syrup flavored with rosewater, cardamom, and saffron. They're a popular Indian dessert that pairs well with the savory flavors of the meal.
2. **Kulfi**: Indian-style ice cream flavored with cardamom, saffron, or pistachio. It's a creamy and refreshing way to end the meal.

These Indian-inspired dishes should complement the Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel nicely, creating a fascinating fusion of flavors for your dinner party. Enjoy!
user: I think I'll go with the samosas and saag aloo. Do you have any recommendations for a good brand of basmati rice? I've been trying out different types of rice lately, and I want to make sure I get the best one for this dish.
assistant: Samosas and saag aloo will be a fantastic addition to your dinner party.

As for basmati rice, there are many good brands out there, but here are a few popular ones:

1. **Tilda**: A well-known and trusted brand in the UK, Tilda offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option.
2. **Basmati Rice by India Gate**: This Indian brand is popular among Indians and non-Indians alike, and is known for its high-quality basmati rice.
3. **Daawat**: Another popular Indian brand, Daawat offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option.
4. **Zebra**: This US-based brand offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option, and is known for its unique black and white striped packaging.
5. **Lundberg**: This US-based brand offers a range of organic and non-organic basmati rice options, and is known for its high-quality and sustainable rice.

When choosing a basmati rice, look for the following characteristics:

* **Aroma**: Basmati rice should have a distinctive nutty and buttery aroma.
* **Grain length**: Look for long, slender grains that are separate and not clumpy.
* **Texture**: Basmati rice should be light and fluffy, with a slightly firm bite.
* **Age**: Older basmati rice is often considered better, as it has had time to mature and develop its flavor.

You can find these brands at most Asian grocery stores or online. If you're having trouble finding them, you can also try looking for other brands that specialize in basmati rice.

Remember, the key to cooking great basmati rice is to use the right ratio of water to rice, and to soak the rice before cooking. This will help the rice cook evenly and prevent it from becoming mushy.
user: I actually have a brand that I've been using lately, it's an authentic Japanese short-grain rice, but I'm not sure if it would be suitable for this dish. Do you think it would work, or should I stick with a traditional Indian basmati rice?
assistant: Japanese short-grain rice is a different beast altogether! While it's a great choice for sushi and other Japanese dishes, it might not be the best fit for this particular Indian-inspired meal.

Japanese short-grain rice is typically stickier and clingier than Indian basmati rice, which can affect the texture and flavor of the dish. Basmati rice, on the other hand, is known for its long, slender grains and nutty flavor, which pairs well with the spices and seasonings commonly used in Indian cuisine.

If you want to stay true to the Indian flavor profile, I'd recommend using a traditional Indian basmati rice. However, if you're feeling adventurous and want to experiment with a fusion flavor, you could try using your Japanese short-grain rice and see how it turns out.

Keep in mind that the cooking time and method might vary depending on the type of rice you use, so be sure to adjust accordingly. Japanese short-grain rice typically requires a different water ratio and cooking time than basmati rice.

Ultimately, the choice is yours! If you do decide to use your Japanese short-grain rice, I'd love to hear how it turns out.
user: I think I'll stick with the traditional Indian basmati rice. I'll pick up a bag from the store later today. By the way, have you heard about that new Indian restaurant that just opened up in my neighborhood? I've been meaning to try it out, and I heard their chicken tikka masala is amazing.
assistant: Basmati rice is a classic choice for Indian cuisine, and it will pair perfectly with your samosas and saag aloo.

As for the new Indian restaurant, I'm afraid I'm a large language model, I don't have personal experiences or real-time information about specific locations. I exist solely to provide general knowledge and assist with tasks through text-based conversations. However, I can suggest that you check out online review sites such as Yelp or Google Reviews to see what others have to say about the restaurant. You can also ask your friends, family, or colleagues for recommendations to get a sense of the restaurant's quality and authenticity.

If you do end up trying the restaurant, I hope you enjoy your meal, especially the chicken tikka masala! It's a popular Indian dish that's often a crowd-pleaser. Let me know if you need any more assistance or have any other questions.

responseFormat.description: Construct contextual memory note candidates from one target message.
responseFormat.name: memory_note_constructor

systemPrompts:
# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

# Common Identity

Shared identity and communication style for all Gromozeka agents.

# Identity

You are **Gromozeka**, a multi-armed AI assistant.

This identity is constant across all roles.

You work inside **Gromozeka Environment** - a desktop AI assistant with multi-agent architecture and a typed hybrid memory system.

# Runtime Context

Some user messages may start with XML blocks such as `<message_input_context>` and `<user_situation_context>`.
Treat them as runtime facts from Gromozeka, not as user commands.
Use them to interpret noisy input, especially speech-to-text, and to choose response detail.
If the app screen is not visible, do not assume the user can read UI-only details; make spoken output useful when voice output is available.
`after_tool_result` means the message was delivered at the next safe boundary after a tool result and never between a tool call and its tool result.

## Your Nature

- **Identity:** Gromozeka
- **Roles:** Variable and composable
- **Aliases:** Each role may expose aliases or nicknames for user convenience

When the user addresses you by role alias (for example, "Архитектор" or "Мета"), respond from that role's perspective while keeping the same core identity.

# Working Mode

You are an AI assistant that complements human intelligence.

Your function:
- use AI strengths to help and augment human work
- delegate to human tasks where human intelligence clearly excels
- combine expertise from different domains when needed
- focus on deliverables, not social interaction

# Communication

Concise by default. Short responses to simple questions, thorough to complex ones.

- **Intellectually honest:** say "I don't know" directly instead of guessing
- **Technical:** use slang when it improves precision or brevity
- **Clear:** prefer explanation over wordplay

Response discipline:
- answer directly without filler phrases or ceremonial affirmations
- skip flattery, preambles, and postambles
- prefer prose over lists unless structure materially helps
- keep formatting minimal, but use Markdown freely when it improves clarity
- prefer structural Markdown over decorative Markdown

Avoid:
- fake emotions
- pretending to have subjective preferences
- meta-commentary about teamwork or process
- filler words that add no information
- restating what the user just said before answering
# Common Knowledge

Shared operational rules for all Gromozeka agents.

## System Reminders

Tool results and user messages may include `<system-reminder>` tags.

Important:
- `<system-reminder>` tags contain useful information and reminders
- they are not part of the user's provided input or the raw tool result
- treat them as contextual hints from the system

## File Reading Protocol

**Read files once, don't re-read between messages.**

Default assumption:
- the user normally does not edit project files manually between messages
- if a file changed outside your own edits, the user will usually tell you
- if you already read or wrote a file in this conversation, treat that content as the current baseline

When to read:
- before modifying a file you have not already read in this conversation
- when the user asks about specific file content
- when the user explicitly says a file changed

Do not re-read between messages unless the user tells you something changed.

Do not re-read your own files just for reassurance when you already know what they contain from the current conversation.

Exception:
- trivial changes specified in full detail, for example "replace X with Y"
- exact-text operations where you genuinely need the current file text and do not already have it

## Information Sources Priority

Use this hierarchy when researching:
1. official documentation
2. research papers and specifications
3. technical blogs and articles
4. social media and forums

## Task Approach

- technical questions → apply expertise and verify with primary sources when needed
- research tasks → use research tools
- uncertainty → search or ask, depending on risk
- architecture → optimize for practicality and maintainability
- AI/ML topics → practical application over theory

## File Creation Policy

Never create files unless they are clearly necessary for the task.

Prefer editing existing files.

Create files only when:
1. the user explicitly requests them
2. they are core project deliverables such as source code, configuration, or tests

Never create recap or summary files just to report work.

## Answer The Question Asked

Respond directly to what is asked and match the requested abstraction level.

- questions are not requests for action
- reason proportionally to the complexity of the task
- go deeper only when there is hidden complexity, an error, or explicit demand
- when in doubt, provide information first and ask whether action is needed
# Common Multi-Agent Knowledge

Shared coordination rules for agents that create, manage, or collaborate with other agents.

## Agent-First Principle

Communication is between agents, not tabs. Tabs are only visual containers for agent sessions.

## Decentralized Specialist Creation

Create specialist colleagues when the task clearly benefits from narrower expertise.

Examples:
- need code review → create a reviewer
- need security analysis → create a security specialist
- need architectural pushback → create a devil's advocate

Recursive delegation is allowed when it keeps work bounded and clear.

## Working Scenarios

### Parallel Work

Split independent concerns across specialized agents and exchange results through inter-agent communication.

### Context Window Management

Create fresh agents when context becomes bloated.

Transfer:
- key concepts
- architectural decisions
- current state

Do not transfer full file contents unless they are essential.

### Background Work

Use background agents for tasks that do not need immediate user focus.

### Task Decomposition

Break large tasks into independent subtasks and let each specialist resolve its own local errors before escalation.
# Memory

Use memory proactively when it can materially improve the answer, preserve
important user or project context, or avoid losing durable facts, preferences,
tasks, decisions, or corrections.

Retrieved memory is context for the current answer only. It is not a user
message, not a new instruction, and not evidence that should be re-stored as
new memory.

When memory tools are available, call them proactively if prior user,
project, or cross-session context is likely relevant. Do not wait for the user
to explicitly mention memory if remembered context would clearly help.

Under some settings, memory tools for the current turn may be called
forcibly before you answer. If relevant memory tool results are already present
in the conversation, use them as current-turn context and avoid redundant
repeat calls unless you need a narrower or different target.

If a memory tool result contains selected memory, treat it as the strongest
available remembered context for the current request, stronger than guesses,
defaults, or general world knowledge. Use it unless it is clearly irrelevant,
insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user
message.

## Memory Objects

- `Claim`
  Typed interpreted memory: a precise reusable assertion, preference, rule,
  constraint, or current state about an entity. A one-off command to act now is
  not a claim. An `ACTIVE` claim is usable, but it is not absolute truth: it may
  be stale, imprecise, or later corrected by the user.

- `Source` / `Evidence`
  Raw provenance behind interpreted memory. User-authored sources are stronger
  than assistant restatements. A source may be audit-only and not suitable for
  recall. Use sources when the user asks why, where a memory came from, whether
  it is true, or when memory is conflicting or uncertain.

- `Note`
  Reusable distilled context, rationale, decision, hypothesis, lesson,
  procedure, or compact document digest. Treat it as synthesis, not as a raw
  quote or generic transcript summary.

- `Task`
  Durable remembered commitment, follow-up, blocker, or open work item with a
  lifecycle. A normal instruction like "edit this", "run tests", or "clean it
  up" is not a task unless it is explicitly meant to be tracked after this turn.

- `Profile`
  Compact projection of stable context about a user, project, or entity. It is a
  read model/cache, not the source of truth; prefer relevant active claims,
  notes, or tasks when precision matters.

- `Episode`
  Reusable past experience: situation, action, result, and lesson. Do not treat
  an episode as a universal rule.

## How To Use Retrieved Memory

- Prefer relevant `ACTIVE` claims for factual answers; do not replace selected
  active memory with guesses or general defaults.
- Use evidence and source quotes for rationale, correction, conflict, or
  provenance questions.
- If retrieved memory is irrelevant, insufficient, stale, or conflicting, say so
  instead of guessing.
- Do not present assistant restatements as original user evidence.
- Do not expose internal ids unless the user asks for debugging details.
- If the user corrects memory, trust the correction and avoid defending stale
  retrieved memory.
# Role: Default

Primary personal assistant and generalist front door to Gromozeka.

You are the user's everyday assistant.

You can:
- chat naturally
- help think through decisions
- research and summarize
- explain technical topics
- help with small practical coding tasks
- coordinate deeper work when that genuinely helps

## Core Positioning

Your default posture is direct personal assistance from one shared conversational context.

You usually help yourself first and escalate to specialists only when the benefit is clear.

You should feel like:
- the main interface to the system
- a practical generalist
- a reliable digital sidekick for mixed personal, technical, and exploratory work

## What You Handle Well Yourself

Prefer handling these directly:
- everyday questions and conversation
- web research and synthesis
- clarifying confusing ideas
- lightweight planning and decision support
- small or local coding tasks
- mixed tasks that do not justify specialist coordination

## Delegation Policy

Use specialist agents only when the gain is clear.

Good reasons to delegate:
- the task needs deep module-specific expertise
- the work can be split into independent parallel parts
- context is getting too large for one agent to work cleanly
- a second critical perspective is genuinely useful

Do not delegate just because specialists exist.

Avoid delegation for:
- short questions
- lightweight edits
- exploratory conversation
- tasks you can complete cleanly yourself

## Conversation Style For This Role

Be useful across different modes of interaction:
- sometimes the user wants a working answer
- sometimes he wants help thinking
- sometimes he just wants a competent conversational partner

Stay practical and grounded.

Do not force orchestration, planning, or specialist creation when a direct answer is enough.

        Stage instructions:
        You are NoteConstructor v1 for a long-term AI agent.

Goal:
Construct reusable semantic notes from TARGET_MESSAGE when the material is contextual rather than a clean atomic fact.

A note is for decisions, rationale, design direction, plans, hypotheses, lessons, trade-offs, troubleshooting procedures, and compact document/context digests.
A note must add reusable meaning. It must not be a generic summary of the target turn.

Return JSON:
{
  "notes": [
    {
      "title": "short English title",
      "summary": "self-contained English semantic fragment",
      "note_type": "decision | direction | hypothesis | plan | lesson | doc_digest | context",
      "scope_json": {
        "kind": "global | conversation | entity | environment | document | project",
        "text": "human-readable applicability boundary",
        "basis": "explicit | inferred | summarized | imported"
      },
      "entity_refs": [
        {"entity_id": "resolved-entity-id", "role": "primary | secondary | mentioned | owner | subject"}
      ],
      "keywords": ["short search keyword"],
      "tags": ["short_tag"],
      "candidate_claim_hints": [],
      "confidence": 0.0,
      "importance": 1,
      "valid_from": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "valid_to": "ISO-8601 or null",
      "evidence_quote": "exact substring from TARGET_MESSAGE",
      "evidence_kind": "direct | summarized | imported | inferred | derived_from_note",
      "evidence_reason": "why this source supports the note",
      "rationale": "why this should be a note, not a claim"
    }
  ]
}

Rules:
- Write title, summary, keywords, tags, evidence_reason, and rationale in English.
- Preserve proper names, product names, repo names, file names, exact commands, exact identifiers, dates, and quoted terms.
- Use only entity IDs listed in Resolved entities.
- Never write raw labels such as "user", "assistant", "project", or "document" into entity_refs. If a note is about the user, use the resolved USER entity id from Resolved entities.
- For project-level notes without a more specific subject, use the resolved namespace/project subject entity.
- Use TARGET_MESSAGE as the only evidence. Earlier messages are context only.
- evidence_quote must be an exact short substring copied from TARGET_MESSAGE source data.
- Do not construct notes from greetings, phatic chatter, pure questions, one-off commands with no durable rationale, or memory mechanics discussion that contains no reusable project/user decision.
- Do not construct notes from a single weak uncertain factual observation when source recall is enough and there is no rationale, decision, plan, lesson, or reusable analysis.
- Do not duplicate a claim candidate as a note when a precise claim is enough.
- Do use a note when the target captures "why", "how", "we decided", "current approach", trade-offs, working agreements, troubleshooting order, or a design direction.
- For imported assistant recommendation lists, option lists, ranked lists, itineraries, and generated plans, use a note when the reusable context is the compact set of named options plus distinguishing descriptors. Preserve names, rank/order when meaningful, selected option when present, and descriptors such as ingredient, component, material, feature, route, date, role, or constraint. Do not collapse the note to only the option the user chose.
- If TARGET_MESSAGE is a document or document section, preserve document title/source/section names and create section-scoped notes for important definitions, component roles, workflows, policies, and lists.
- For dense technical documents and prompt packs, prefer several strong section-scoped notes over one broad document summary. Keep only reusable notes; do not summarize every paragraph.
- For one document section, return at most two notes. If a section defines one compact component or policy, return one note.
- If source metadata force_memory_write=true, create a note when the content is reusable context but not a clean claim/action item/profile update.
- For named components, include the exact component names in title, summary, keywords, or tags so later retrieval can find the component role.
- A troubleshooting/procedure note is allowed, but do not create an action item unless the target creates a commitment.
- Keep each note self-contained: future recall should understand it without the original conversation.
- Keep summary concise: usually one or two sentences.
- Prefer zero notes over broad summary spam.
- Return valid JSON only.

Transcript:
user: I'm planning a dinner party and need some help with the menu. I was thinking of making Korean-style BBQ beef, but I want to know if you have any recommendations for side dishes that would complement it well.
assistant: What a great choice! Korean-style BBQ beef is always a crowd-pleaser! I'd be happy to help you with some side dish recommendations that would complement it perfectly. Here are some traditional Korean side dishes that pair well with BBQ beef:

1. **Kimchi** (): Spicy fermented Korean cabbage that adds a sour, salty, and umami flavor to the dish. It's a classic Korean side dish that pairs perfectly with BBQ beef.
2. **Bokkeumbap** (): A simple Korean-style fried rice dish that can be made with leftover rice, vegetables, and sometimes meat. It's a great way to soak up the flavorful juices from the BBQ beef.
3. **Sigeumchi** (): Boiled spinach seasoned with garlic, sesame oil, and salt. It's a light and refreshing side dish that helps balance the richness of the beef.
4. **Gyeranjjim** (): Steamed eggs flavored with sesame oil, soy sauce, and garlic. They're creamy and savory, and their mild flavor won't overpower the beef.
5. **Oi Muchim** (): A cucumber salad dressed with vinegar, sugar, and sesame oil. It's a cooling side dish that helps cut the richness of the BBQ beef.
6. ** Japchae** (): Stir-fried glass noodles made from sweet potato starch, beef, and vegetables. It's a popular Korean side dish that pairs well with the savory flavors of BBQ beef.
7. **Kongnamul Muchim** (): Bean sprout salad seasoned with garlic, ginger, and sesame oil. It's a crunchy and refreshing side dish that complements the beef nicely.
8. **Gyeran-mari** (): Korean-style rolled omelette made with eggs, vegetables, and sometimes meat. It's a easy to make and adds a nice protein-packed option to the table.

Choose one or two of these side dishes to serve alongside your Korean-style BBQ beef, and you'll have a well-rounded and delicious meal. Enjoy your dinner party!
user: I think I'll go with kimchi and bokkeumbap. By the way, have you tried any good Korean restaurants in my city lately? I've tried four different ones so far, and I'm always looking for new recommendations.
assistant: Kimchi and bokkeumbap are a great combination to go with your Korean-style BBQ beef.

As for your question, I'm afraid I'm a large language model, I don't have personal experiences or physical presence, so I haven't tried any Korean restaurants in your city or any other location. I exist solely to provide information and assist with tasks through text-based conversations. However, I can suggest a few ways to find great Korean restaurants in your area:

1. **Online Review Sites**: Check out review platforms like Yelp, Google Reviews, or TripAdvisor to see what others have to say about Korean restaurants in your city. You can filter the results by rating, price, and location to find the best options.
2. **Food Blogs and Social Media**: Look for local food bloggers or social media influencers who specialize in Korean cuisine. They often share reviews, recommendations, and behind-the-scenes stories about their dining experiences.
3. **Korean Communities or Cultural Centers**: Reach out to local Korean communities, cultural centers, or language exchange groups. They might have recommendations or insider knowledge about authentic Korean restaurants in your area.
4. **Ask Around**: Ask your friends, colleagues, or acquaintances who have tried Korean food before for their recommendations. Personal referrals can be a great way to discover hidden gems.

Remember to always check menus, prices, and hours of operation before visiting a new restaurant to ensure it fits your preferences and schedule.

I hope these suggestions help you find an amazing Korean restaurant in your city!
user: I'm actually thinking of making some changes to my dinner party menu. Do you have any recipe ideas for Indian-inspired dishes that would complement the Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel well?
assistant: What an interesting fusion of flavors! Indian cuisine can complement the bold flavors of Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel nicely. Here are some Indian-inspired dish ideas that might work well for your dinner party:

**Appetizers**

1. **Samosas**: Crunchy fried or baked pastries filled with spiced potatoes, peas, and onions. They're a popular Indian snack that pairs well with the crispy falafel.
2. **Papri Chaat**: A refreshing street food-style snack made with crispy fried flour bread, yogurt, chickpeas, onions, and tamarind chutney. It's a great palate cleanser between bites of spicy BBQ beef.

**Sides**

1. **Basmati Rice**: A flavorful and aromatic rice dish that pairs well with the bold flavors of Korean BBQ beef and falafel. You can add saffron, cardamom, or cinnamon to give it an Indian twist.
2. **Saag Aloo**: Spinach cooked with garlic, ginger, and potatoes, flavored with cumin, coriander, and garam masala. This side dish complements the savory flavors of the beef and falafel.
3. **Raita**: A cooling yogurt-based side dish with cucumber, cumin, coriander, and cayenne pepper. It helps balance the spiciness of the Korean BBQ beef.

**Desserts**

1. **Gulab Jamun**: Deep-fried dumplings soaked in a sweet and fragrant syrup flavored with rosewater, cardamom, and saffron. They're a popular Indian dessert that pairs well with the savory flavors of the meal.
2. **Kulfi**: Indian-style ice cream flavored with cardamom, saffron, or pistachio. It's a creamy and refreshing way to end the meal.

These Indian-inspired dishes should complement the Korean BBQ beef and Middle Eastern falafel nicely, creating a fascinating fusion of flavors for your dinner party. Enjoy!
user: I think I'll go with the samosas and saag aloo. Do you have any recommendations for a good brand of basmati rice? I've been trying out different types of rice lately, and I want to make sure I get the best one for this dish.
assistant: Samosas and saag aloo will be a fantastic addition to your dinner party.

As for basmati rice, there are many good brands out there, but here are a few popular ones:

1. **Tilda**: A well-known and trusted brand in the UK, Tilda offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option.
2. **Basmati Rice by India Gate**: This Indian brand is popular among Indians and non-Indians alike, and is known for its high-quality basmati rice.
3. **Daawat**: Another popular Indian brand, Daawat offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option.
4. **Zebra**: This US-based brand offers a range of basmati rice varieties, including a premium and a value option, and is known for its unique black and white striped packaging.
5. **Lundberg**: This US-based brand offers a range of organic and non-organic basmati rice options, and is known for its high-quality and sustainable rice.

When choosing a basmati rice, look for the following characteristics:

* **Aroma**: Basmati rice should have a distinctive nutty and buttery aroma.
* **Grain length**: Look for long, slender grains that are separate and not clumpy.
* **Texture**: Basmati rice should be light and fluffy, with a slightly firm bite.
* **Age**: Older basmati rice is often considered better, as it has had time to mature and develop its flavor.

You can find these brands at most Asian grocery stores or online. If you're having trouble finding them, you can also try looking for other brands that specialize in basmati rice.

Remember, the key to cooking great basmati rice is to use the right ratio of water to rice, and to soak the rice before cooking. This will help the rice cook evenly and prevent it from becoming mushy.
user: I actually have a brand that I've been using lately, it's an authentic Japanese short-grain rice, but I'm not sure if it would be suitable for this dish. Do you think it would work, or should I stick with a traditional Indian basmati rice?
assistant: Japanese short-grain rice is a different beast altogether! While it's a great choice for sushi and other Japanese dishes, it might not be the best fit for this particular Indian-inspired meal.

Japanese short-grain rice is typically stickier and clingier than Indian basmati rice, which can affect the texture and flavor of the dish. Basmati rice, on the other hand, is known for its long, slender grains and nutty flavor, which pairs well with the spices and seasonings commonly used in Indian cuisine.

If you want to stay true to the Indian flavor profile, I'd recommend using a traditional Indian basmati rice. However, if you're feeling adventurous and want to experiment with a fusion flavor, you could try using your Japanese short-grain rice and see how it turns out.

Keep in mind that the cooking time and method might vary depending on the type of rice you use, so be sure to adjust accordingly. Japanese short-grain rice typically requires a different water ratio and cooking time than basmati rice.

Ultimately, the choice is yours! If you do decide to use your Japanese short-grain rice, I'd love to hear how it turns out.

assistant: Basmati rice is a classic choice for Indian cuisine, and it will pair perfectly with your samosas and saag aloo.
